# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Movies with great banter

**Score:** 62
**Body:** Movies with characters who perhaps love each other, or hate each other, and show it by listening and disagreeing intently.  

I don't mean something like _The Man from Earth_, which is dialogue based, but specifically _banter_, it should be humorous dialogue.  Something like _Rosencrantz & Guildenstern Are Dead_.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Nice Guys and Kiss Kiss Bang Bang from Shane Black.

Layer Cake from Matthew Vaughn
2. Any Coen Bros movie, but specifically O Brother... and The Big Lebowski
3. In Bruges
4. See Kevin Smith...
5. His Girl Friday
6. Lock Stock and Two Smoking Barrels: that’s good for banter!
7. Pulp Fiction
8. Honestly, The Princess Bride is full of great banter, scene after scene
9. *The Social Network* has some good banter.
  - I once saw someone say Aaron Sorkins dialog is written as if everyone had a half an hour to prepare their response.

## 2. I need actually crazy documentaries

**Score:** 55
**Body:** Whenever I look up crazy or impactful documentaries I always get the same suggestions.
Like Gabriel Fernandez, 77 Minutes, and all of the same stuff. 
I want like insane plot twist or something heart breaking like Dear Zachary. 
Please give me suggestions that are crazy! 
Thanks in advance :) 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Class Action Park
2. Werner Herzog is your man
3. Omg- no one said Grey Gardens?
4. Everyone has probably seen it, but *Abducted in Plain Sight* on Netflix. 
Also really liked *The Fox Hollow Murders* on Hulu.
5. Alpinist
6. 

Three identical strangers

Tickled

God knows where I am
  - +1 for Tickled. That doco is wonderful, weird and unsettling
7. The Act of Killing
8. Grizzly Man
9. Icarus

## 3. I Love You To Death (1990) Is the most "slept on" perfect movie of all time.

**Score:** 43
**Body:** Directed by guy that wrote Raiders Of The Lost Ark

Produced by Owner of Philadelphia Eagles

Stars: Kevin Kline, Tracey Ullman, William Hurt, River Phoenix, Keanu Reeves

Plot: Woman tries to murder cheating husband. Sounds like nothing special. 

Twist: This shit really happened. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Great movie!
2. One of the FUNNIEST movies ever made!!!!
3. Very good movie
4. One of my favorites, perfect casting!
5. Kevin F-ing Kline is a boss!
6. Thanks. What?
7. Ladies, monopoly!
8. I adore this movie!
9. A porch classic
10. I often think of Kevin Kline dancing the the Hall and Oates song. Was it “You make my Dreams come true?”

## 4. Miller's Crossing

**Score:** 34
**Body:** I've watched a lot of films but I've never heard tighter dialogue in any of them. There's not a word wasted and every sentence conveys great detail. 

It's been my favorite film for over 20 years and despite my best efforts I can't find one that matches it. 

This post was intended to suggest this film but if anyone else shares my passion for this film please share your other favorite films. 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Chinatown (1974)
2. One of the greatest gangster films ever made imho
3. If you’re looking for some safe bets start here:

Barton Fink.

Blood Simple. 

The Man Who Wasn’t There. 

True Grit. (This century)
  - blood simple is so good!
4. The film was loosely based on The Glass Key (42). Have you seen that one?
5. VERY different movies but five I love with great dialogue -

Glengarry Glen Ross

Who's Afraid of Virginia Woolf

Sexy Beast

In Bruges

The Sweet Smell of Success
6. It ain't elves...
7. I really like Gabriel Byrnes character. How the story around him unfolds.
  - He was givin' me the high hat!

I love all the derogatory expressions. Jon Polito's performance... I could go on.
8. Brick has some amazingly tight dialogue and a similar lean noir vibe. It's like a Raymond Chandler detective story taking place in a modern high school setting. It's got a great wry sense of humor similar to Miller's Crossing and there's the same sort of dame and tough guy interplay. It's criminally overlooked.

## 5. Suggest me a movie that feels cold.

**Score:** 31
**Body:** I saw [this post](https://www.reddit.com/r/interestingasfuck/comments/1op9xs9/this_is_what_antartica_looks_like_in_62_degrees/?utm_source=share&utm_medium=web3x&utm_name=web3xcss&utm_term=1&utm_content=share_button) and now I feel like watching a movie that will make me feel the cold in my bones.

I've already seen:

* Society of Snow
* Anatomy of a Fall
* 30 Days of Night
* The Revenant
* The Day After Tomorrow
* The Hateful Eight
* Snowpiercer
* The Wolf of Snow Hollow
* Frozen and Frozen 2
* Ice Age
* The Shining
* The Adventures of Nuktuk: Hero of the South
* Misery
* Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind
* Into the Wild
* Murder on the Orient Express
* The Chronicles of Narnia: The Lion, The Witch, and the Wardrobe

Got anything for me? **Preferably not something about murder. Not feeling murdery.**

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Thing
2. Let the Right One In
3. I can’t believe no one has said Fargo! 🥶 There is murder but so much more!
4. The Grey
5. The Ice Storm
6. The Terror
7. Wind River
8. Alive
9. Winter's Bone
10. Dr. Zhivago. THAT will make you cold.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Movies that hit addiction right on the head with how accurate they depict it?

**Score:** 1467
**Body:** Movie is Flight (2012)

I’ve definitely struggled with alcohol over the past few years. Sometimes I can go months before I go on a bender for a weekend. But when I do go on one, I regret it. Hard not to when you don’t have anything to look forward when the bottle is empty. 

But, while my parents don’t know about my habits, there was a time where I downed a glass of Jack Daniel’s in just a few minutes and got up and poured myself more without even thinking, and them staring at me with a mix of 😳 and 🤨 expressions, and I just laughed them off. 

Watching this movie at times was like looking into a mirror and realizing how much I was able to drink without much thought or effort. Maybe I’ll fix myself, maybe not. 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Haven’t had a drink in 7 months after 40 years of recreational drinking. Not drinking is the easy part. Trying to figure out how to spend leisure time is another thing altogether.
  - Yes, bad habits fill the time. When you quit you’re left with confronting all the cool stuff you told yourself you would do so now do it.
  - I'm almost 17 months sober.  

It's sad to say this, but my life is less fun now.  I used to go out and do stuff with friends.  Even when there wasn't shit to do you could make the mundane fun by getting loaded.  

It is what it is.  Grown man shit.  Better than being hungover every day until I'm drunk.  Or spending $15 a day on booze.  Or having a fat fucking puffy booze bag face.

Bright side, I feel about 150% better on average every day.  Lost 40lbs without even trying.  Saved a LOT of money I didn't even know I had.  Blood pressure is back to good and I'm even donating blood again.

That got off subject.  Sorry.

Edit-goddamn thanks for the replies guys.  I feel firm in not drinking, and I’m grateful for that every day.  I guess I’m lucky my (last) bottom was low enough to terrify me out of temptation.

I will say I’m proud that I went to a dance party on Halloween.  It was a parade/dance thing in SF and I was extremely anxious going into it about being sober.  But I pulled up my pampers and got dressed up and I fucking danced in the streets with my lovely partner.  Then we went to dinner and I drank a coke like a good boy.  Then I went home and ate some candy and went to bed and slept real human sleep and in the morning when I woke with the sun I felt good.  And fuck I’m grateful for that.  Kind of a sobriety IYKYK sorta thing.
    - Sobriety ain’t easy. I’m 3yrs. 

Less fun? In a way, yes.
Safer and overall better? Oh fuck ya.
2. Trainspotting.
  - Requiem For A Dream hits really hard; especially the last 30 min.

Also Leaving Las Vegas for sure.
3. Not a movie but Sharp Objects captured the reality of alcoholism
  - Sharp Objects stayed with me for a looooong time.
4. Hey buddy I'm right there with you so I get it. Just remember every day is a new day and try not to be too hard on yourself.
5. Leaving Las Vegas

## 2. Name a movie where the first 10 minutes had you completely hooked!

**Score:** 1071

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Blade....the bloodbath club scene.

Baby Driver

Dark Knight
  - Bellbottoms!
2. Saving Private Ryan.
3. Kung fu hustle.
4. The Dark Knight.
5. The Other Guys
6. 28 Weeks later. Hands down.
7. Mad Max: Fury Road
8. Are you gonna name that film OP?
  - Dungeons and Dragons

## 3. Probably one of the coldest scenes in cinema history , what y'all think ?

**Score:** 309
**Body:** There are scenes that make you feel the power before a single word is spoken — this one from Star Wars : Rogue One still gives chills. The lighting, the silence, the sheer presence… pure menace captured in one frame.
Would you say this is one of the coldest entrances in cinema history, or do you have another scene that tops it? 👀🔥

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. totally agree,  that scene is pure power. You can feel the fear and respect in the air before he even moves. One of the best villain entrances ever.
2. Yeah... I saw it in the theater with my whole family and (being a Star Wars nerd my whole life) this scene wrecked me. I think I was weeping with joy infront of my wife and kids the whole way home and never was able to articulate how powerful it was. Movie magic.
3. This pushed Rogue One right up there! It was a decent solid 7/10 film, then this happened and it became my favourite Star Wars film since the originals!
4. Can already hear the theme .. 

john williams is an all time 🐐.
  - I don't know if I can back up this hot take but John Williams' score for those films is a huge reason Star Wars is where it is. Like, imagine watching it for the first time and then BAM you see Star Wars and then a space battle!
5. You can feel the fear before he even swings the saber.
6. This brought on woo hoos and cheers in the theater even one guy screamed "fuck yeah".. Its what movies are all about
7. It would have been even more awesome if this was Vaders first scene and he wasnt advertised for being in the movie
  - You were not summoned here to grovel Director Krennic
8. Will be much much colder if it's actually Jar Jar Bonks hiding under that mask.

## 4. MacGruber. This whole movie is hilarious and underrated but this exchange cracks me up everytime. What are your underrated comedies?

**Score:** 302

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. MacGruber is an amazing film. Val Kilmer at his best
  - One of my favorite parts is when Mac removes the guidance system from the nuke and Val just goes "weeeeak!"
  - Let’s go pound some Cunth
2. “Just tell me what you want me to fuck!”
3. This and Pop Star: Never Stop Never Stopping
4. The two sex scenes had me dying.
  - I’m gonna *shoooooooot*
5. Tucker and Dave vs Evil
6. Macgruber has the team set up and waiting in the vehicle. Boom!
7. Will Forte is a treasure!

## 5. I just finished watching Band of Brothers. I want realistic heart wrenching Holocaust movies please.

**Score:** 297
**Body:** Listen I know it may be a strange request but I would like recommendations on Holocaust films that show the gruesome truth. It's hard to watch but important for me.

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The pianist, Schindler’s list
  - I'd suggest *Jakob The Liar*, *Everything is Illuminated*, and *Life is Beautiful*.
    - I'd suggest Jakob the Liar as the last movie to watch about the Holocaust. Watch it, but as a pallet cleanser.
  - The Zone of Interest shows normalising the brutality that was Auschwitz, from the camp commander's family home, next to the camp.
  - Boy in the striped pajamas
2. Gonna get flak for it from some but, Life is Beautiful got me then, gets me now.
3. The Zone of Interest
4. Shoah
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shoah_(film)
5. It's not a Holocaust Film in any sense.    
But the single most gut-check WW2 Movie I've ever seen is Come and See.    
It's about a Central European town....that has the Germans pull out and then the Soviets pull in.    
And it's......there's not a single bad scene...it's not technically a perfect movie, but the story is just incredible.    
And it happened over and over and over and over.....
6. [Escape from Sobibor.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=M_shvPLSAz4) Warning: will crush your soul.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. What’s the earliest instance of someone being tortured to diegetic pop music in film?

**Score:** 33
**Body:** Saw Bugonia, which promoted this question. These kind of scenes almost always seem to work in a perversely delightful way. Earliest I can think of is Reservoir Dogs but I assume there’s got to be something earlier. 

I wouldn’t quite count something like the Clockwork Orange “Singin’ in the Rain” scene. I’m talking about someone popping in a record/tape/cd/etc. 

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I mean, later on in A Clockwork Orange that guy who had to hear singin in the rain puts on the symphony that triggers the main character's ptsd from the torture he was subjected to.
  - Clockwork Orange (1971) was the first to come to mind for me.

There's the scene in Touch of Evil (1958) where the men terrorize and presumably rape Janet Leigh with pop music playing, diegetic iirc.

In the Big Combo (1955), Richard Conte puts a hearing aid in Cornel Wilde's ear and blares jazz from a radio into it as a form of torture.
    - _Touch of Evil_, you’re right, yes it’s diegetic, it’s playing there.
2. I think Clockwork Orange counts personally and is probably where Tarrantino got the idea from. 


I can't think of one that fits your criteria exactly before Reservoir Dogs, but Death and the Maiden (1994) kinda completely hinges on this idea. Sigourney Weaver attempts to work out if her unannounced houseguest, Ben Kingsley, is the man who used to torture her years previously whilst playing a specific opera. Underrated movie.
3. Before I subjected myself to movies/scenes like Reservoir Dogs, Funny Games or even 
the  “digital hells” that are prevalent in more than a few *Black Mirror* episodes there was Sonny and Chers, “ I Got You Babe” from “A Groundhog Day”. 

That’s torture.. right?
  - Come to think of it, Funny Games does the opposite of this in its opening. The extremely caustic, almost grindcore John Zorn song that plays while they take a pleasant drive.

I never thought of it, but it must be referencing this trope.
4. Many cite Kenneth Anger as the first to do ‘ironic pop music’. Theres no explicit person turning the music on and torturing another, but Scorpio Rising creates the dichotomy of violence with it that is the basis
5. I don't know if it's the earliest, but my favorite is in the 1974 Afrofuturist/blacksploitation film "Space is the Place." About experimental jazz musician Sun Ra, who played in real life and in the movie, an enlightened being from Saturn. In the movie, he's kidnapped by NASA, tied up and forced to hear the South's anthem, "Dixie." Watch the whole movie if you've never seen it. [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=owCPrIEliZc](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=owCPrIEliZc)
6. James Cagney's 1961 comedy "One, Two, Three" has communist characters being tortured with "She wore an itsy bitsy teenie weenie yellow polka dot bikini"

The thing is though, it's just the song itself that's supposed  to be torturing them... Not sure if that counts or it could be a more original concept where eventually torture was added to the pop music to up the shock value...

Personally, my guess is the trope originated with the idea of governments using music for psychological warfare.
7. lol this is such an interesting and bizarrely specific question. my gut says it must have been french new wave, though i can’t think of an example. it’s just a very french new wave thing to do.

## 2. Stanley Kubrick’s success despite his reclusiveness

**Score:** 24
**Body:** Is Stanley Kubrick’s personality and lack of promotion in film unique? He didn’t seem to promote any of his movies, do interviews, or anything like that, despite his legendary status in the 1970s and 1980s. He also appeared to have carte blanche to make whatever movies he wanted. Nearly every other director with that level of acclaim is visibly present in public.

Like this guy would go almost a decade between films yet studios still gave him near complete control. Maybe Terrance Malick is a good parallel? I just see directors like Scorcese and Tarantino out there all the time interviewing and talking about their work and it’s seem wild to me that the GOAT himself just didn’t seem interested in that kind of stuff. 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. He wasn’t a recluse at all. He just had complete control on access. He was known to be contact constantly with countless people on the phone all the time. He maintained long intense working relationships with countless creatives and would call and talk to them all the time.

He had the Warner Bros top brass and countless directors on call. He could literally call experts on AI across the world to do a three way call with him and Spielberg while working on AI.

He frequently installed state of the art fax machines with any of his working companions. He just hated flying and was a homebody. He didn’t want to leave UK and he liked the lack overtsight of Hollywood and living on his bombass country estate with his wife, daughters and animals.

There was a funny thing where like a bunch of  people were all saying they talked to him on the phone the day he died. Like he was constantly in contact with collaborators and artists he respected.

He wasn’t a recluse he just managed access.
2. He was actually very concerned and on top of the marketing of his films. And the reclusive and mysterious image was one he was consciously playing into as a way of creating intrigue. And it worked well, people see him as a reclusive genius and perfectionist and often forget that he was just a dude. A very intelligent and talented dude but the god like mysterious image he had was just very good self marketing.
  - He also for the most part simply did not agree with the idea of informing an audience what he as the artist felt the meaning of a piece of work was especially not in the medium of television or print where it would be taken as definitive. 

In contrast to this there's some fantastic correspondence in the Kubrick archival collection you can see on request at the UAL in London where ordinary people contact Stanley with ideas about what the film meant to them etc. His responses are often very receptive to it and a few times he basically offers to try and arrange the opportunity to discuss their interpretation together in person. I believe at least one of those letters relating to 2001 is reprinted in the Taschen book on Kubrick.
3. A lot of the “mad genius” stuff about Kubrick was just marketing. He *was* a perfectionist who generally embraced ambiguity which lended credence to the idea, and now it’s become cinema (pop) history.
  - It's unfortunate how many movies of his are subsumed by his mystique rather than being taken for what they are.
4. He had a whole warehouse full of boxes containing all the materials used for his work--including promotional materials.  He was very involved in the promotion process down to measuring the precise space of ads for his films in newspapers.

Warner Bros. was also 100% behind him.  At a certain point, his success, time in between films, and (as others have said) his reclusiveness was apart of the promotion behind his films.

He also wasn't public because he most likely then had to answer to rumors about himself so I doubt interviews would be much about the films at all--and he was notorious for not wanting to explain anything about his films anyway, so that's another reason.

> He also appeared to have carte blanche to make whatever movies he wanted

[The doc. explains it pretty well.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ApEh9Sm4BR0&t=1hr22m11s)  I recommend it.
5. The secret is that his reclusiveness was part of the promotion of his films - if made each one an event and something to be picked over. It’s either strategy or just his personality, but was probably a combination of both.
  - We don't have many stars anymore, everybody is also an influencer showing us everything that happens in their every day. Celebrities lost their appeal when they lost their mystery.
6. Kubrick earned his reclusiveness. In fact he wasnt really known for being that reclusive until full metal jacket. He wasn't reclusive early in his career. 

Studios like him because he made great movies that usually came in under budget despite their long shoot times. He worked with a very small crew on most movies to keep the costs down.
  - Even FMJ he did interviews for. OP could look up any of the books about him, or his collected interviews books, he talks about his work. Except EWS do obvious reasons. 
And yeah, he kept an eye on his budgets. I can’t recall the film, maybe shining, but he kept tabs on the Orange juice being consumed during filming.

## 3. Paris, Texas (1984)

**Score:** 4
**Body:** **Just a bit of why background, why I'm posting:**

Never posted on here...or many subreddits in general, but, as a major movie-buff myself, I admittedly was a late-comer to this movie...but I feel like writing, apologies if it's too long or meandering. ***Thanks for anyone who's interested in my views...curious to see what other viewpoints there are out there? As this is particularly not a movie many people I know - even have interest in - so I appreciate the opportunity to hear viewpoints on this.***

**--** 

First read about this in a book, probably late teens - in that "1001 movies" etc. book list...I was a little drawn to it, only because it so plain, but curious as to why it was considered so great. I think seeing it on a top ten list on YouTube prompted me to finally put it on, and at first I just enjoyed falling asleep to it, which I do often...but hearing the dialogue:

***I finally watched it from beginning to end - with full attention:***

***My Personal Understanding/Summary:***

Paris, Texas is about one man alone - seeking and finding redemption, reconciliation, forgiveness, and complete absolution from his past. This man, Travis, is the story itself and the point of the film - beginning and ending with him - the only "story" that unfolds is a result of what happens to Travis as he himself unfolds. This seeking and finding, I believe, is as if the audience is witnessing the final pilgrimage of the Soul...a renewed spirit, "back from the dead", and ill explain my perspective:

Travis at first is appears obscure, plain, everyday looking man - stripped bare of identity, wandering from the likewise bare, vast, unknown desert...speechless to the point of uncivilized, yet nonetheless interesting and, paradoxically - more interesting than his chatty brother, who try's to reconnect with Travis and integrate him back into society, social context, and civilization. 

But society, or civilization are not what Travis ultimately enters back into - they have no meaning to him, or the story - it is family that Travis finds connection, meaning in; family which becomes a the setting and stage of the man unfolding. He is seemingly unwitting, reluctant initially... this isn't even of his own violation, but it's only his brother Walt - and his reaching out, reuniting him, and act of brotherly love - that is very important. 

Why? Because although were witnessing a the journey of one man, the story of one soul - family becomes absolutely necessary for the meaning and context of that journey - and "family" can't be portrayed by one man's acts alone...There is a step that can't be taken by Travis himself - family becomes one "landscape" in which Travis and his story unfolds, not society. 

Family is at the very least the second most important theme in here, portrayed as something sacred, meaningful in every level - as opposed to society - which merely serve as a secondary backdrop...I personally love the way it is portrayed realistically, not melodramatic - very naturalistic:

For example, the "old-videos" scene: that light, peaceful music...old memories...most people can relate to this. I was surprised how softly moving and meaningful I found this particular scene, especially when Travis is revealed to have a deep inner love/pain for his estranged wife, Jane. I think any male, or anyone really, can relate to that kind of sadness when remembering lost love and estrangement from obviously in this case: the one, or "true love". But it's captured brilliantly with Travis seemingly sighing, sinking into himself with closed eyes as if the pain of losing Jane is more than he can bare, yet it's authentic love, and also - the Father-Son/Lost-Wife dynamic unfolds here...

***I'll finish up:***

***The final scenes are a beautiful and poignant culmination of a very gentle story, the monologue Travis dives into is the "big reveal" and climax of his journey: he truly talks, and opens up with Jane.... We hear him expressing himself more so and more authentically than the rest of the movie, as this quite long, cathartic release is an outpouring of the heart...romantic, without becoming too gooey, or melodramatic, or typical of any genre. It's actually quite not a particularly happy ending. I would summarize it as the full reveal of Travis's journey: a man of true transcendence of ego, selfishness, immorality, expressing what he realized meant most to him as he has come "full circle" in his journey.***

***Travis’s decision to let go of Jane and Hunter is an act of painful acceptance and selflessness... I did feel a bit sad for Travis, truly wanted him to be with Jane and Hunter, like most viewers I'd imagine. In my view one of the most moving and beautifully captured scenes: Jane walking in the hotel room and embracing Hunter, pure joyous reconnection, silent with no dialogue - a deeply moving, joyous love being expressed/contrasted with finally with Travis driving away.***

***In a strikingly self-transcendent and remarkable act for any man, and a father: lost, heart-broken likely, but resolve of his past, his guilt, his "sin". A bittersweet ending and why I would call it a "spiritual" film: since it is dealing with the most important, virtuous, highest ideals and loftiest expressions a man can realize in himself. Yet told in such a plain, simple, true-to-life, unique way.***

  
\--- **So that concludes my personally written view/review of the movie. Thankyou for anyone who read all of that, and I'd love to hear other views...or perspectives (prove my view ridiculous if you have to)--**



### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 4. Tulse Luper Suitcases

**Score:** 3
**Body:** Has anyone been able to see the three films that make this multi media project by Peter Greenaway? From what I gather, the films seem to divide fans, with some feeling that it is the ultimate Greenaway experience and others feeling that it is so impenetrable/dense that it feels quite alienating. 

For those who have seen these films, I am curious to learn more about them.

What did you enjoy/dislike about them? How challenging were these films? Was it worth the trip? I am assuming this was only ever shown at Key events/ film festivals. What was it like to watch them in real time? Will there ever be a theatrical release??

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I'm a massive Greenaway fan, so I'm happy to talk about these...

**DISCLAIMER: I AM ABOUT TO SPOIL A FAIRLY UNIQUE IN-PERSON EXPERIENCE AT A MUSEUM IN LOS ANGELES. DON'T READ ON IF YOU THINK YOU'LL EVER GO TO THE MUSEUM OF JURASSIC TECHNOLOGY**

In the Palms/Culver City area of LA, there is this little museum nestled in an old building off of Venice Boulevard, called the Museum of Jurassic Technology. There isn't much information available about the museum online. The website looks like it hasn't been updated since 1998. Reviews tend to be cryptic, ranging from "I didn't get it" to "This is the greatest museum I have ever been to". 

On the inside, the museum is a dimly lit, tightly packed maze of micro exhibits, each generally no bigger than a few walls, a room at most. None of the exhibits feel particularly interconnected. Topics range from the story of a rare South American bat with an amazing talent, called the Deprong Mori, to a several pieces of machinery apparently capable of alchemy, to a painting gallery honoring the first canine cosmonauts launched into space.

The crowning centerpiece (and probably largest segment) to the main collection, however, is an exhibit dedicated to one Geoffrey Sonnabend. At great length and detail, the exhibit walks you through Sonnabend's life, beginning a generation back, with the material ambitions of his father, who was trying to build a bridge over a dangerous river. The exhibit meanders through the details of this endeavor, and an entire offshoot room is dedicated to the life of Madalena Delani, a tragic Romanian-American opera singer, whose only relevance to the story of Geoffrey Sonnabend is that he heard her sing at a pivotal moment in his life. Finally, after exploring every detail of Sonnabend's life, without so much as a hint at what the point of it all is, the exhibit abruptly changes course and explains how Sonnabend's real fame comes from his theory of memory, portrayed as something called the 'Cone of Obliscence' or the 'Cone of True Memory'; essentially, a 3D model rendering the shape of human interactions with our own memories.

The actual dynamics of how the cone works are hilariously complicated but do almost make sense. Or, maybe, it only *feels* that way because you, as the audience, have become so thoroughly hypnotized by the soporific, convoluted mess that is the preempting material about Sonnabend's life.

This is the whole trick of the Museum of Jurassic Technology. Geoffrey Sonnabend isn't a real person. His entire life and life's work is a fabrication.

Indeed, nothing in the museum is true. Or maybe some of it is?

The point of the museum, I would argue, is to engage the audience on a specifically metatextual level. To ask the audiences to accept everything and nothing at the same time. Partially, the museum works just as a joke, where you just let the experience wash over you, laugh at the inane details and absurd pseudo-science. But the museum also works as a commentary on museums, themselves. It is arranged much like a 17th century cabinet of curiosities, a precursor to the modern museum; items of suspect origin and dubious veracity are portrayed with the same level of reverence and relevance that a Da Vinci painting might be. The whole point being to remind audiences to question why they react to things a certain way.

All of this is a roundabout way of saying, the Tulse Luper Suitcases function in a similar way.

I have seen all three of the main films, though not in their original museum exhibition settings. They are available online if you know where to look. And in reality, this is only a fraction of what Tulse Luper has to offer; the actual complete exhibition is far more than just the three films, and the other multimedia elements (like pictures of the limited run exhibits) are much harder to find. And divorced from their original context, probably lack some of the same punch. I have also not seen the fourth, follow-up film, *A Life in Suitcases*.

Watching the Tulse Luper films is like seeing an exhibit at the Museum of Jurassic Technology. They are presented with the same sort of freeform style that calls back to museum multimedia exhibits and PBS Sunday morning mini-docs. Layered text and sound, picture-in-picture presentation, commentary by both characters and outside objective (subjective?) observers. It is almost like they are not supposed to be watched intently, so much as they are meant to be merely 'observed'. Casually absorbed while walking past. If you were to drop in and out of each film at, say, 30 minute intervals, you might feel like you were watching a completely different film each time.

Watching them like movies, though, *is* a fascinating experience. Textually, they land somewhere in the realm of Pynchon, Heller, Vonnegut, and DeLillo. The characters are inscrutable, with motivations that range from simple and obsessive, to complicated and ridiculous. The "hero", himself, is a fifth business: he is just a guy who likes making lists, who ends up in more increasingly complex and absurd prisons, for reasons generally beyond his control. He exerts very little force on the narrative, but then, the narrative wouldn't exist without him.

So yes, I would say that the films, as I have experienced them, are practically gobbledygook. They are nonsensical, tangential, and difficult. I also find them to be quite the trip, which feels like the point to me. They are also filled with some fantastic little performances, from the likes of Ana Torrent, Roger Rees, Jordi Molla, and Nigel Terry (in one of his last film roles), just to name a few.

I sincerely doubt that these films will ever get any serious re-examination. We'd be extremely lucky if they even maybe got included as a special feature on a different Greenaway release (the way *The Falls*, a sort of precursor to *Tulse Luper*, is included on the blu ray for *A Zed and Two Noughts*).
  - Wow! Thank you so much for your response. Both the exhibition and the films sound like quite an experience. How does Tulse Luper compare to his other films? I've  read that it's his most ambitious work. I am also a big fan of The Falls, so I am guessing Tulse Luper is somewhat similar?
    - I would say, in some ways, they feel like a notable bridge between his earlier work (say, *Draughtsman* through *The Baby of Macon*), and his more obscure later work (like *Nightwatching* and *Eisenstein in Guanajuato*).

They have that kind of digital alienness that pervades all of his later work, but still also has the kind of blue humor, frank sexuality and mortal grotesquery of his earlier work. All packaged with his usual elevated, tableau style. 

I wouldn’t say it’s particularly *more* difficult than something like *Drowning By Numbers*. It’s certainly not *Prospero’s Books*, but then again, that’s basically just an avant garde art film that could just have easily been a Kenneth Anger piece as it could Greenaway. 

I just think the Tulse films are long, especially taken as a whole (all three parts clock in at like 6 hours combined), and that’s why people label them as “difficult”. 

I’d say it’s best to just digest them as three separate parts, don’t get too caught up in trying to make sense of the narrative, and remember one of the key refrains from the films: there is no history, only historians.
      - Thanks for the extra information. Very intrigued by this project. I hope to see them someday

## 5. Being There Ending - Peter Sellers Walking on Water

**Score:** 3
**Body:** Here's the scene: https://youtu.be/Bow1ZJTV4L4

So I just watched Being There after many years and was blown away by the not only the film, but especially the final moments. The most logical explanation for me is that it's a submerged platform. It's such a perfect, ambiguous ending (even though I embrace the one that reinforces the theme of perception).

A few takes on the ending:

**1. The Mystical/Religious Interpretation**

This is the most direct reading. Because the act of walking on water is an explicit Biblical allusion -- Chance is a kind of "holy fool" or a modern-day Messiah. His improbable rise from simple gardener to political deity is seen as the miracle itself from a  man who pretty much reflects back the wisdom people project onto him.

**2. The Metaphorical Interpretation**

This approach focuses less on a divine miracle and more on a philosophical statement. It often cites the President's line heard during the scene: "Life is a state of mind." Since Chance is completely unburdened by societal expectations or self-doubt, he literally doesn't know he can't walk on water, and therefore, he does. This is seen as his ultimate transcendence—achieving a pure state of simply "being."

**3. The Literal Interpretation (a submerged platform)**

This is the one I buy -- the scene isn't actually supernatural at all. Instead, Chance is just walking on a submerged dock, a shallow spot in the water, or maybe some cleverly hidden support under the ice.

The whole point is how the camera fools us into seeing a miracle. This is the ultimate dig by the movie: the audience, just like the ridiculous politicians in the film, instantly chooses to believe the grand, impossible story over the simple, logical explanation of it being a submerged dock. It reinforces the entire theme that perception is everything, and people will project deep meaning onto something completely empty.

* [Source - Being There Ending Explained](https://screenrant.com/being-there-movie-ending-explained/)

* [Source - Being There: American Cipher](https://www.criterion.com/current/posts/4473-being-there-american-cipher)

* [Source - Walking on Water: Decoding the Enigmatic Ending of Being There](https://screenplayhowto.com/screenplay-blog/walking-on-water-decoding-the-enigmatic-ending-of-being-there/)

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The whole point is that there isn't any definite answer. Chance is such an empty vessel that everyone he encounters projects their own expectations and desires onto him. He's like a mirror, nothing there but the reflections of who's in front of him. That's why there's the spiritual motif at the end with him walking on water. He has the sunyata of Buddha or the kenosis of Christ.
  - And I'm doing the same thing with my interpretation, reading into the film's meaning is an act of projection on my own part. Chance is simply "being there"
    - From the same Roger Ebert article I linked in my other comment:   
  
*So what does it show us? It shows us Chance doing something that is primarily associated with only one other figure in human history. What are we to assume? That Chance is a Christ figure? That the wisdom of great leaders only has the appearance of meaning? That we find in politics and religion whatever we seek? That like the Road Runner (who also defies gravity) he will not sink until he understands his dilemma?*

*The movie’s implications are alarming. Is it possible that we are all just clever versions of Chance the gardener? That we are trained from an early age to respond automatically to given words and concepts? That we never really think out much of anything for ourselves, but are content to repeat what works for others in the same situation?*

*The last words in the movie are, “Life is a state of mind.” So no computer will ever be alive. But to the degree that we are limited by our programming, neither will we. The question is not whether a computer will ever think like a human, but whether we choose to free ourselves from thinking like computers.*
2. From Roger Ebert's Great Movies Article on this film: [https://www.rogerebert.com/reviews/great-movie-being-there-1979](https://www.rogerebert.com/reviews/great-movie-being-there-1979)

*In the much-discussed final sequence of “Being There,” Chance casually walks onto the surface of a lake. We can see that he is really walking on the water, because he leans over curiously and sticks his umbrella down into it.*

*When I taught the film, I had endless discussions with my students over this scene. Many insisted on explaining it: He is walking on a hidden sandbar, the water is only half an inch deep, there is a submerged pier, etc. “Not valid!” I thundered. “The movie presents us with an image, and while you may discuss the meaning of the image it is not permitted to devise explanations for it. Since Ashby does not show a pier, there is no pier–a movie is exactly what it shows us, and nothing more,” etc.*

It is less important to try to explain the image or to try to "decode" it than it is for you interrogate the thoughts and feelings it brings up in you. A movie doesn't exist until it is seen and processed by an audience - what the audience takes from it is just as valid as what the director intended. So what did you take from it?
